comments.text: At related OEIS sequences it says 'A146968 possible'. Does that mean that even more sequences are possible, or is it a weird glitch? I noticed that a similar thing happens at https://www.erdosproblems.com/979.

The meaning of "possible" given at https://github.com/teorth/erdosproblems/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md is "there may be a theoretically computable sequence associated to the problem that is in the OEIS and not already listed on the table; but it needs enough values actually computed that one can cross-check with that database."
Yes - in general the 'possible' tag is left to indicate that there seem to be more OEIS sequences that could be added here - which is certainly the case here.

Similar to Brocard's problem [398], Cushing and Pascoe proved that there are finitely many powerful numbers "close to factorials", i.e. for given $k$, $|x - n!| \le k$ has finitely many integer solutions $(x, n)$ where $x$ is powerful (arxiv), assuming ABC conjecture. Similarly, this arxiv paper shows that, for given coprime integers $k, r$ there are finitely many powerful numbers of the form $k^n + r$, again assuming ABC conjecture (Theorem 2.3). So the answer is positive conditionally. Note that both references are found by GPT-5.

Some powerful numbers of these forms are: \begin{equation*} 2^1 - 1 = 1 \end{equation*} \begin{equation*} 2^3 + 1 = 9 = 3^2 \end{equation*} \begin{equation*} 2! - 1 = 1 \end{equation*} \begin{equation*} 4! + 1 = 25 = 5^2 \end{equation*} \begin{equation*} 5! + 1 = 121 = 11^2 \end{equation*} \begin{equation*} 7! + 1 = 5041 = 71^2 \end{equation*} I don't know of any others. Interestingly, half of these are also solutions to [398].
